elements.sent-2411-001: The electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) is a quantum interference phenomenon capable of altering the optical response of a medium, turning an initially opaque atomic sample into transparent for a given radiation field (probe field) upon the incidence of a second one (control field).
elements.sent-2411-002: EIT presents several applications, for instance, considering an atomic system trapped inside an optical cavity, its linewidth can be altered by adjusting the control field strength.
elements.sent-2411-003: For the single-atom regime, we show that there is a fundamental limit for narrowing the cavity linewidth, since quantum fluctuations cannot be disregarded in this regime.
elements.sent-2411-004: With this in mind, in this work we also investigate how the linewidth of an optical cavity behaves for different numbers of atoms trapped inside it, which shows a quantum signature in a strong atom-field coupling regime.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition, we examine how the other system parameters affect the linewidth, such as the Rabi frequency of the control and the probe fields.
elements.sent-2411-006: INTRODUCTION Cavity electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT) is a remarkable phenomenon in quantum optics that has attracted significant attention due to its potential applications in quantum information processing and precision measurements \cite{werner1999photon, zhang2007slow .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In EIT experiments, a weak probe field can be transmitted through an otherwise opaque optical medium when a strong control field is applied on it \cite{RevModPhys.77.633 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The seminal work of Jaynes and Cummings \cite{JCM laid the theoretical foundation for EIT, paving the way for its subsequent experimental realization, also with remarkable controllability at the quantum level as using a single atom inside an optical cavity \cite{PhysRevLett.105.153604, mucke2010electromagnetically .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The studies of EIT in atomic three-level systems in $\Lambda$-configuration have been theoretically and experimentally investigated \cite{PhysRevLett.66.2593, RevModPhys.77.633 with applications in slow-light experiments \cite{hau1999light , quantum memories \cite{PhysRevLett.86.783, liu2001observation , cooling of trapped atoms \cite{PhysRevLett.85.4458, PhysRevLett.85.5547, PhysRevA.93.053401 and many others, thus making EIT an important tool for the development of second-generation quantum technologies.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: EIT arises under specific conditions involving the Rabi frequencies of the probe ($\Omega_p$) and control ($\Omega_c$) fields, leading to the coherent population trapping (CPT) phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-011: This is characterized by the emergence of a dark state, defined as an atomic state decoupled from the light fields, consisting of a coherent superposition of the two ground states of the atomic system in the $\Lambda$-configuration \cite{agap1993coherent .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Under the condition $|\Omega_p|\ll |\Omega_c|$, the absorption of a weak probe field tuned resonantly with some atomic transition is suppressed \cite{RevModPhys.77.633 , giving rise to a significant optical nonlinearity in the susceptibility and a corresponding change in the refractive index \cite{PhysRevLett.105.153603, PhysRevLett.64.1107, lukin2001controlling .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This nonlinearity has then been used for several applications, for example to control the linewidth of optical cavities, allowing to obtain linewidths much smaller than the natural ones of the cavities \cite{ficek1997spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this work, an investigation of the fundamental limits to cavity linewidth narrowing in the realm of cavity EIT is presented.
elements.sent-2411-015: Analyses of how light-mediated interactions and spontaneous decays affect the width of the transparency window has already been made theoretically \cite{Lukin:98, borges2017influence, PhysRevA.104.063704 and experimentally \cite{Wang:00 .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Unlike previous works, here we employ a quantum master equation approach to analyze the system dynamics and the behavior of the narrowing of the cavity linewidth considering various parameters, such as the number of trapped atoms ($N_{\text{at }$), the atom-field coupling strength ($g$), and the probe field amplitude ($\varepsilon$).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Our findings shed light on the interplay between these parameters and the linewidth of the transmitted probe field, providing insights into the optimal conditions for achieving narrower linewidths and controlling the photon statistics in this system.
elements.sent-2411-018: The structure of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: Section \ref{sec:PHYSICAL SYSTEM AND MODEL presents the open quantum system model and the numerical methods employed to solve it.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Section \ref{sec:TRANSMISSION IN CAVITY-EIT explores the system transmission profile, examining the cavity linewidth and atomic populations according to the parameters that affect the atom-cavity response.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The main results on cavity linewidth and photon statistics as functions of the number of trapped atoms are presented in Sec.~\ref{sec:LINEWIDTH DEPENDENCE WITH THE NUMBER OF ATOMS , while Sec.~\ref{sec:CONCLUSIONS covers the conclusions.}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width = 1.0\textwidth, clip]{figures/fig1.pdf} \caption{(a) Cavity-EIT setup with $N_{\text{at}}$ atoms coupled to the cavity mode. A probe field drives the cavity, while a control field drives the atoms. (b) $\Lambda$-type three-level atom with two ground states ($\ket{1}$ and $\ket{2}$) and an excited one ($\ket{3}$). The transition $\ket{2} \leftrightarrow \ket{3}$ is driven resonantly by the control field with frequency $\omega_c$, while the transition $\ket{1} \leftrightarrow \ket{3}$ is resonantly coupled to the cavity mode with frequency $\omega$. Moreover, a probe field, with frequency $\omega_p$, pumps the cavity mode with a detuning $\Delta_p = \omega - \omega_p$. (c) Normalized transmission spectrum of the cavity with $N_{\text{at}}=1$. Throughout this work, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) is expressed in units of the cavity field decay rate $\kappa$. For stronger control field strengths ($\Omega_c=5\kappa$) the transmission spectrum becomes equivalent to an empty-cavity scenario $(\text{FWHM} = 1)$, on the other hand we have the cavity-EIT spectrum for $\Omega_c = \kappa$. (d) FWHM dependence with $\Omega_c$ for different number of atoms. Here we set the probe field strength $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$, atom-field coupling strength $g=\kappa/\sqrt{N_{\text{at}}}$ and atomic spontaneous decay rates $\Gamma_{31}=\Gamma_{32}=0.5\kappa$.} \label{fig:scheme}
elements.sent-2411-022: We consider $N_\text{at}$ three-level noninteracting atoms confined into a two-sided optical cavity, with one of its sides pumped by a coherent probe laser.
elements.sent-2411-023: Another external classical control field drives the atoms.
elements.sent-2411-024: Figure \ref{fig:scheme (a) provides a pictorial illustration of the experimental setup and Fig.~\ref{fig:scheme (b) shows the $\Lambda$-level atomic configuration.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The ground state $\ket{1}$ couples resonantly to the excited state $\ket{3}$ via the intracavity (quantum) mode with frequency $\omega$ and coupling strength $g$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Meanwhile, the control field (frequency $\omega_c$) induces a Rabi frequency $2\Omega_c$ between the second ground state $\ket{2}$ and the excited state $\ket{3}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Finally, a probe field, with frequency $\omega_p$, pumps the cavity mode with a driving strength $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Within the electric dipole and rotating-wave approximations, the time-independent Hamiltonian (in a frame rotating with the probe frequency $\omega_p$) that describes the atom-cavity system is ($\hbar= 1$) \cite{PhysRevLett.111.113602 \begin{equation}H = \Delta_pS_{11}-\Delta_pa^\dagger a + (\varepsilon a+ gaS_{31} + \Omega_c S_{32} + \text{H.c.}), \label{Hamiltonian}\end{equation} where $\Delta_p = \omega - \omega_p$ is the probe-cavity field frequency detuning, $S_{ij = \sum^{N_{\text{at _{k=1 \ket{i ^{(k) \bra{j ^{(k) = \sum^{N_{\text{at _{k=1 \sigma_{ij ^{(k)}$ are the collective raising and lowering atomic operator for $i\neq j$, and atomic energy-level population operators for $i=j$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Respectively, $a$ and $a^\dagger$ are the photon annihilation and creation operators, while $\text{H.c.}$ stands for Hermitian conjugate.
elements.sent-2411-030: The system dynamics, including the dissipative and decoherence effects at $T = 0 \, \text{K}$, is determined by the master equation \cite{PhysRevLett.111.113602 \begin{eqnarray}\dfrac{d \rho}{d t} = -i[H, \rho] + \dfrac{\kappa}{2}(2a\rho a^\dagger - a^\dagger a\rho - \rho a^\dagger a) \nonumber + \sum\limits_{k=1}^{N_{\text{at}}} \sum\limits_{l=1}^2 \left[\dfrac{\Gamma_{3l}}{2}\left( 2\sigma_{l3}^{(k)}\rho\sigma_{3l}^{(k)} - \sigma_{33}^{(k)}\rho - \rho\sigma_{33}^{(k)} \right) \right] \nonumber + \sum\limits_{k=1}^{N_{\text{at}}} \sum\limits_{j=1}^2 \left[\dfrac{\gamma_{j}}{2} \left( 2\sigma_{jj}^{(k)}\rho\sigma_{jj}^{(k)} - \sigma_{jj}^{(k)}\rho - \rho\sigma_{jj}^{(k)} \right) \right], \label{ME}\end{eqnarray} where $\rho$ represents the density matrix of the atom-cavity system, $\kappa$ is the decay rate of the intracavity field intensity, and the rates for atomic spontaneous decay and dephasing are, respectively, $\Gamma_{3l}$ ($l=1,2$) and $\gamma_j$ ($j=1,2$).}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\dfrac{d \rho}{d t} = -i[H, \rho] + \dfrac{\kappa}{2}(2a\rho a^\dagger - a^\dagger a\rho - \rho a^\dagger a) \nonumber + \sum\limits_{k=1}^{N_{at}} \sum\limits_{l=1}^2 \left[\dfrac{\Gamma_{3l}}{2}\left( 2\sigma_{l3}^{(k)}\rho\sigma_{3l}^{(k)} - \sigma_{33}^{(k)}\rho - \rho\sigma_{33}^{(k)} \right) \right] \nonumber + \sum\limits_{k=1}^{N_{at}} \sum\limits_{j=1}^2 \left[\dfrac{\gamma_{j}}{2} \left( 2\sigma_{jj}^{(k)}\rho\sigma_{jj}^{(k)} - \sigma_{jj}^{(k)}\rho - \rho\sigma_{jj}^{(k)} \right) \right], \label{ME}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-031: The system dynamics is obtained numerically by solving the master equation in the steady-state regime.
elements.sent-2411-032: Employing the Monte Carlo method \cite{Molmer:93 , with a number of trajectories large enough to reproduce the mean values derived through the master equation~\footnote{In our simulations, we observed that more than 2000 trajectories is already enough to reproduce the master equation mean values.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Thus, we have fixed 2560 trajectories in our simulations, being this number chosen in order to facilitate the use of parallel processing in the multicore cluster we have available.
elements.sent-2411-034: This limitation arises due to the exponential growth of the dimension of the density matrix $\rho$, $\text{dim =M\times M$, where $M=3^{N_{\text{at N$ \cite{nation2015steadystatesolutionmethodsopen , thus limiting the numerical solution to a few atoms.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: By solving the master equation, one numerically calculates the normalized transmission spectrum of the cavity $\langle a^\dagger a \rangle/|\varepsilon/\kappa|^2$, atomic populations $\langle \sigma_{ii \rangle$, normalized second-order correlation function $g^{(2) (0)=\langle a^\dagger a^\dagger a a \rangle/\langle a^\dagger a \rangle$, and the mean value of photon number projection operators $ P_n = \langle \ket{n}\bra{n \rangle$, thus allowing us to investigate the nonlinear effects in our system.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The degree of nonlinearity can be quantified by the cooperativity parameter defined as $C\equiv N_{\text{at g^2/2\kappa \Gamma$, being $\Gamma=\Gamma_{31 +\Gamma_{32}$ the total decay rate of the atomic excited state.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, the inverse of $C$ gives the critical number of atoms required to significantly influence the system transmission.
elements.sent-2411-038: For two-level systems, we can also define the critical number of photons, $n_c=\Gamma^2/2g^2$, interpreted as the minimal number of photons needed to change the radiation properties of the atom \cite{RevModPhys.Rempe .}
elements.sent-2411-039: To deal with a larger number of atoms, other techniques or approximations must be employed.
elements.sent-2411-040: As detailed in Appendix \ref{SC , the semiclassical approximation allows us to solve the coupled differential equations for a large $N_{\text{at }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: This is possible since we can treat the cavity mode as a classical field with a time-dependent amplitude.
elements.sent-2411-042: The Rabi frequency of the control field, $2\Omega_c$, significantly influences the atom-cavity response of the system.
elements.sent-2411-043: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:scheme (c), for $N_{\text{at =1$ (single atom) and $\Omega_c \gg g$, the system exhibits an empty-cavity behavior.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: However, when $\Omega_c\approx g$, the characteristic transmission spectrum of cavity-EIT emerges.
elements.sent-2411-045: This spectrum features a central peak at the resonance between the probe field and the cavity mode.
elements.sent-2411-046: The two secondary peaks correspond to the dressed Jaynes-Cummings states \cite{JCM , which are located in $\pm \sqrt{g^2 + \Omega_C^2}$ \cite{PhysRevLett.111.113602 .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Now, examining the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the central peak, it is possible to observe how it depends on the Rabi frequency of the control field and the number of the atoms trapped inside the cavity for a probe field with a fixed average photon number ($\langle n \rangle$).
elements.sent-2411-048: This can be observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:scheme (d), but the difference between the linewidths starts to become prominent when $g \ge \kappa$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Otherwise, when $g<\kappa$ the FWHM is independent of $N_{\text{at }$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In particular, by keeping the effective atom-cavity mode coupling constant ($g_{N_{\text{at = g \sqrt{N_{\text{at }$), as we increase $N_{\text{at }$, the minimum value of the FWHM decreases.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: This indicates that, although the effective atom-field coupling and the Rabi frequency of the control field remain the same, the minimum FWHM still depends on the number of atoms trapped inside the cavity.
elements.sent-2411-052: This occurs because the greater the number of atoms, the more photons from the probe field can be absorbed or influenced by the atomic system.
elements.sent-2411-053: For weak probe fields (with negligible two-photon probability), only a single atom would be enough to modify the properties of the cavity transmitted field, allowing us to reach a very small FWHM.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, by increasing the intensity of the probe field, a single atom would no longer be capable of altering the properties of the probe field.
elements.sent-2411-055: These behaviors can be observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:map , which depicts the impact of the probe field intensity on the system with a single trapped atom.}
elements.sent-2411-056: We plotted the FWHM as a function of $g/\kappa$ and $\Omega_c/\kappa$ considering the maximum average number of photons inside the cavity $(\langle n \rangle = |\varepsilon/\kappa|^2)$ as $\langle n \rangle=0.01$ [Fig.~\ref{fig:map (a)] and $\langle n \rangle=0.1$ [(Fig.~\ref{fig:map (b)].}}
elements.sent-2411-057: For smaller $g$ or $\Omega_c$, the empty-cavity behavior remains.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the limit of $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$, the FWHM theoretically tends toward zero.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[trim = 2.8mm 1.5mm 1.5mm 1mm, width = 0.240\textwidth, clip]{figures/FWHM_x_g_Oc_Nat1_ep001_art.png} \includegraphics[trim = 1.0mm 1.5mm 2.5mm 1mm, width = 0.235\textwidth, clip]{figures/FWHM_x_g_Oc_Nat1_ep01_art.png} \caption{Full width at half maximum (FWHM) as a function of $g/\kappa$ and $\Omega_c/\kappa$ for $N_{\text{at}}=1$. Here we set the probe field strength as (a) $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.01}\kappa$ and (b) $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$, with atomic spontaneous decay rates $\Gamma_{31}=\Gamma_{32}=0.5\kappa$.} \label{fig:map}
elements.sent-2411-059: The crucial difference in the low- and high-excitation regimes discussed above for a single atom can be explained in more detail by looking at the atomic populations.
elements.sent-2411-060: At low excitation, e.g., with a weak probe field with a maximum average photon number equal to $0.1$, the FWHM decreases as the atom-field coupling strength $g$ increases [Fig.~\ref{fig:projection_FWHM (a)].}
elements.sent-2411-061: The nonlinear behavior of the linewith was already experimentally confirmed in \cite{Wang:00 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: This narrowing is corroborated by the dynamics of atomic populations ($\langle \sigma_{11 \rangle$ and $\langle \sigma_{22 \rangle$) for states $\ket{1}$ and $\ket{2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: These dynamics show low photon absorption by the atom, but yet sufficient, resulting in low populations in states $\ket{2}$ and $\ket{3}$.
elements.sent-2411-064: In contrast, for a high-excitation probe field, for instance with an average photon number of $1.0$ [Fig.~\ref{fig:projection_FWHM (b)], the strong coupling regime $g\gg \kappa, \Gamma$ promotes the population exchange $\left \langle \sigma_{11 \right\rangle \rightarrow \left \langle \sigma_{22 \right\rangle$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Even in the presence of strong absorption, the FWHM exhibits transparency due to reduced occupations in $\ket{1}$ and $\ket{3}$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[trim = 1.5mm 1.5mm 1.4mm 0.0mm, width = 0.243\textwidth, clip]{figures/Projectionsxg_Nat1_ep01_Oc1_Dp05.pdf} \includegraphics[trim = -2.0mm 1.5mm 2.1mm 1.2mm, width = 0.232\textwidth, clip]{figures/Projectionsxg_Nat1_ep1_Oc1_Dp05.pdf} \caption{Atomic population $\langle \sigma_{11} \rangle$ and $\langle \sigma_{22} \rangle$ of states $\ket{1}$ and $\ket{2}$, and FWHM as a function of the normalized atom-field coupling strength $g/\kappa$ for $N_{\mathrm{at}}=1$, $\Delta_p=0.5\kappa$, $\Omega_c=\kappa$ and $\Gamma_{31}=\Gamma_{32}=0.5\kappa$, considering (a) $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$ and (b) $\varepsilon=\sqrt{1.0}\kappa$.} \label{fig:projection_FWHM}
elements.section-2411-004: Dependence of Linewidth on the Number of Atoms
elements.sent-2411-066: In this section, we examine how the number of atoms influences the transmission of the cavity, building on our previous discussions.
elements.sent-2411-067: Figure \ref{fig:EIT_FWHM illustrates the FWHM as a function of $\Omega_c/\kappa$ for various $N_{\mathrm{at }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We are able to solve the complete quantum master equation for up to $N_{\text{at =5$, while the case of $N_{\text{at =1000$ was derived using semiclassical equations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: For $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$, there exists a significant probability of having two photons in the cavity mode, which cannot be effectively influenced by a single atom.
elements.sent-2411-070: This accounts for the large FWHM observed for all values of $\Omega_c/\kappa$ in the single-atom scenario.
elements.sent-2411-071: As the number of atoms increases, we notice a ``staircase'' behavior in the FWHM because of the possibility to absorb a higher number of photons.
elements.sent-2411-072: This finding is consistent with the predicted linewidth scaling of $\Omega_c^2/N_{\text{at }$ for $N_{\text{at }\ge 3$ as presented in \cite{mucke2010electromagnetically .}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The inset plot shows a decrease in the minimum FWHM values with an increasing $N_{\text{at }$, suggesting a fundamental limit to linewidth narrowing within the quantum framework.}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, the semiclassical approximation \cite{PhysRevA.2.336 presented in Appendix \ref{SC enables numerical simulations for a larger number of atoms (e.g., $N_{\text{at =1000$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: This approach predicts no transmission even in the empty cavity regime ($\Omega_c \ll g$), which deviates from the quantum model.
elements.sent-2411-076: The coupling regime considered in Fig.~\ref{fig:EIT_FWHM , $g=5.0\kappa/\sqrt{N_{\text{at }$, results in $C\gg1$, where a single atom is enough to significantly influence the cavity transmission \cite{RevModPhys.Rempe , as we indeed observed here.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[trim = 1.4mm 1.8mm 1.5mm 1.0mm, width = 0.48\textwidth, clip]{figures/EIT_FWHM_g5_ep01_article.pdf} \caption{FWHM as a function of normalized Rabi frequency of control field $\Omega_c/\kappa$. We consider $N_{\text{at}}=1$ to $5$ for the quantum model and $N_{\text{at}}=1000$ using the semiclassical approximation. In the inset we show the minimal values of FWHM for each number of atoms confined in the cavity. Here we set $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$, $g=5.0\kappa/\sqrt{N_{\text{at}}}$ and $\Gamma_{31}=\Gamma_{32}=0.5\kappa$.} \label{fig:EIT_FWHM}
elements.sent-2411-077: Finally, we analyze the photon statistics of the system in order to get more insights about its behavior as we increase the number of atoms.
elements.sent-2411-078: To this end, we analyze the normalized second-order correlation function $[g^{(2) (0)]$, and the photon number distribution, which are show in Fig.~\ref{fig:statistics for $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$ (weak probe field) and different values of $g$.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Also, the Rabi frequency of the control field $\Omega_c$ is adjusted to minimize the linewidth, so the detuning is $\Delta_p=\text{FWHM /2$.}
elements.sent-2411-080: With these parameters, the photon statistics can be calculated for a considerable probability of one-photon distribution $P_1$ and just a single atom that can influence the cavity transmission in a fundamental limit.
elements.sent-2411-081: Panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:statistics shows $g^{(2) (0)\approx 1$, for the weak coupling regime ($g=0.1\kappa$), no matter how many atoms we have, thus revealing a coherent behaviour, as expected since in the weak coupling regime the atoms are unable to modify substantially the statistical properties of the field.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: On the other hand, when the atom-field coupling is increased, the field stay less coherent, scaling the correlation function.
elements.sent-2411-083: Interestingly, when the number of trapped atoms increases, the system behavior transitions the correlation function close to 1 for bigger values and returns to 1 with a large number of atoms, which is indeed expected since the semiclassical approximation becomes valid in the limit of $N_{\text{at \rightarrow \infty$ \cite{carollo2024applicability .}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This behavior is expected for many systems with a regime of strong coupling interaction, showing a quantum effect in the correlation function.
elements.sent-2411-085: Looking at the distribution of photons $P_n$, in Fig.~\ref{fig:statistics (b) we note that, for $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$, i.e., a probe field with maximum average photon number equals to 0.1, there is a decrease of $P_1$ as increases the number of atoms.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Such decrease is related to the strong atom-field coupling ($g>\kappa, \Gamma_3$) because the critical number of photons ($n_c$) and atoms ($1/C$) will be at least 1 to produce a meaningful change in the field transmission.
elements.sent-2411-087: Then, having a decreasing $P_n$ when increasing the number of atoms in the cavity ($N_{\text{at =1 \rightarrow 2$) significantly changes the photon distribution and consequently decreases all probabilities.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: In the case of a weak atom-field interaction (empty-cavity regime), it does not have a significant change in the field photon distribution.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{centering} \includegraphics[trim = 1.2mm 1.0mm 1.0mm 1.1mm, width = 0.48\textwidth, clip]{figures/g20_x_Nat_FWHMmin_ep01_art.pdf} \includegraphics[trim = 1.2mm 2.0mm 1.0mm 1.0mm, width = 0.48\textwidth, clip]{figures/Pn_x_Nphotons_FWHMmin_Nat_g5_ep01.pdf} \par\end{centering} \caption{(a) Normalized second order correlation function as a function of the number of atoms $N_{\text{at}}$ considering the weak ($g<\kappa, \Gamma_{3}$), intermediate ($g\approx\kappa, \Gamma_{3}$), and strong ($g > \kappa, \Gamma_{3l}$) atom-field coupling regimes. (b) Photon distribution ($ P_n$) and the respective ratio $P_{21}= P_2 /P_1 $ for $N_{\text{at}}$ atoms, and $g=5.0\kappa/\sqrt{N_{\text{at}}}$. Here we set $\varepsilon=\sqrt{0.1}\kappa$, $\Gamma_{31}=\Gamma_{32}=0.5\kappa$, $\Omega_c$ that results in minimal values of FWHM for the respective $N_{\text{at}}$, and $\Delta_p=\text{FWHM}/2$.} \label{fig:statistics}
elements.sent-2411-089: Here we investigated a system composed by single-atoms trapped inside an optical cavity, in which the Rabi frequency of the control field is manipulated in order to provide ideal conditions for EIT phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-090: We also analyzed the linewidth of the transmission spectrum (FWHM) for 1 to 5 atoms where the regimes of low and high excitation and coupling qualitatively changes the system's transmission.
elements.sent-2411-091: A single atom does not significantly impact the system’s transmission in a high-excitation regime of the probe field, as quantified by low photon absorption in the atomic populations.
elements.sent-2411-092: This is evidenced by the population exchange between ground states $\ket{1}$ and $\ket{2}$.
elements.sent-2411-093: On the other hand, in the low-excitation regime with strong atom-field coupling, the occupation of states $\ket{2}$ and $\ket{3}$ is minimal.
elements.sent-2411-094: Our results reveal a fundamental limit for the FWHM in the quantum model, as the linewidth narrows with an increase in the number of atoms linked to photon absorption.
elements.sent-2411-095: Notably, in a semiclassical approximation, the system’s dynamics show a no transmission even for a near-zero control field intensity, which differs from the quantum model.
elements.sent-2411-096: The results presented in this work highlight the advantages of Cavity EIT for applications in quantum memories, specifically due to its capacity for quantum interference.
elements.sent-2411-097: The EIT phenomenon enables the storage and on-demand retrieval of the quantum state of light in a strongly coupled atom-cavity system.
elements.sent-2411-098: While traditional quantum memory setups generally employ an ensemble of many atoms \cite{LEI_Review_EIT , here we show that a small number of trapped atoms already yields significant effects relevant to quantum memory applications, as previously demonstrated experimentally in \cite{specht2011single .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Using single atoms reduces inevitable losses and increases storage reliability via state detection.
elements.sent-2411-100: This paper may contribute to the understanding of how the number of atoms ought to affect the efficiency of quantum memories and, moreover, be applied in quantum state storage protocols.
elements.sent-2411-101: To possibility the simulation of large $N_{\text{at }$ even with the dimension of Hilbert space unable to be solved on high-processing computers, the semiclassical approximation can be applied the weak atom-field coupling regime ($g<\kappa, \Gamma)$.}
elements.sent-2411-102: In Fig.~\ref{fig:EIT_FWHM the simulation for $N_{\text{at =1000$ showed an almost vanishing linewidth for $\Omega_c<\kappa$, but the coupling strength employed does not satisfy the applicability of the approximation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: For obtain the temporal evolution through the master equation it is necessary using the property $\langle \dot{O}\rangle = \text{Tr}\left({\dot{\rho O \right)$ for any atomic or cavity field operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-104: By approximating the atom-field correlations as $\left\langle a S_{ij \right\rangle \approx \alpha \left\langle S_{ij \right\rangle$, where $\alpha$ represents the time-dependent amplitude of the cavity field, we effectively simplify the system by ignoring the direct impact of these correlations on the field.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: This approximation enables the application of this approach to a set of coupled nonlinear differential equations, as discussed in \cite{PhysRevA.2.336 .}
